metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `merged_log_commit` in the HTTP handler contract: state what commit it represents (e.g. the HEAD of the op-cert log branch after the signed envelope is appended/merged), whether the handler performs that merge itself or returns the pre-existing HEAD, and update AC1's round-trip test to assert its value.
findings.finding: `merged_log_commit` appears in the response schema with no explanation of what it contains, how the handler computes it, or whether the handler is expected to perform a log-branch merge. An executor cannot implement or test this field.
findings.location: HTTP handler contract — Response schema; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the handler contract: '`merged_log_commit` is the git commit SHA of the op-cert log branch HEAD after the handler appends (merges) the signed DSSE envelope to that branch; if the gate FAILs and no envelope is produced, return the current HEAD unchanged.' Then add an assertion in AC1's test that verifies this field equals the expected post-merge commit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC5 with a concrete proving command: define 'wedges' (e.g. 'blocks indefinitely on a network call or raises an unhandled exception'), and specify the test as: invoke the stdio rebar-mcp path with no network access (e.g. `unshare -n` or a mocked remote) and assert it completes within N seconds without network I/O.
findings.finding: AC5 ('the default local path stays offline (stdio `rebar-mcp`) and never wedges an op') uses the undefined term 'wedges an op' and provides no concrete proving command, leaving verification open-ended.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(test)' with: 'Test: run the stdio rebar-mcp gate locally with all network interfaces blocked (e.g. `unshare -n pytest tests/test_local_path.py`) and assert it exits cleanly within 30 s — proving no HTTP/DNS call is introduced by the new HTTP handler code path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit constraint that the Ed25519 private key stored in Secrets Manager must have no OpenSSH passphrase (key generated with `-N ''`), since `sshsig.sign()` feeds pae_bytes on stdin and `ssh-keygen -Y sign` cannot interactively prompt for a passphrase in that configuration; document this in the IaC/operator notes.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies that the Ed25519 private key stored in Secrets Manager must be passphrase-free (no OpenSSH passphrase). The existing sshsig.sign() invokes `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path> -n <namespace>` with pae_bytes as stdin and capture_output=True. If the materialized key file has an OpenSSH passphrase, ssh-keygen will attempt to read the passphrase interactively — stdin is already occupied by pae_bytes and there is no terminal, so the subprocess hangs or fails, breaking signing silently. The KMS CMK encrypts the secret at rest inside Secrets Manager; it does not remove an OpenSSH passphrase from the key material itself. The plan must require the stored key to be passphrase-free at the OpenSSH level.
findings.location: Context section — 'Signing key storage + materialization' paragraph; AC line 3 ('materializes it to a 0600-mode temp file … passes that path to sshsig.sign')
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Signing key storage + materialization' paragraph, add: 'The Ed25519 key stored in Secrets Manager must have no OpenSSH passphrase (generated with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N \'\''`); Secrets Manager + KMS CMK is the at-rest encryption layer, not an OpenSSH passphrase.' The IaC test for the container image (AC4) should also assert the key-generation runbook uses `-N ''`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the derivation rule for `merged_log_commit` in the HTTP handler contract — state which git ref/branch it reads, which operation (e.g., `git rev-parse` on the tickets branch after the authoritative fetch) produces it, and at what point in the handler flow it is captured.
findings.finding: `merged_log_commit` is referenced in both the response schema and the DSSE subject binding as a server-computed authoritative value, but the plan never specifies HOW the server derives it — which branch/ref it reads, which git operation produces it, or at what point in the handler flow it is computed. This is a processing protocol gap co-located with a security-critical schema field.
findings.location: Context — HTTP handler contract / Response schema; AC1; AC2 (integrity test)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the trusted server obtains its `principal` (env_id) string — e.g., as an environment variable set by IaC, co-stored with the Secrets Manager secret, or declared in `infra/` — and document that it must exactly match the `env_id` in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`.
findings.finding: The `principal` (env_id) argument required by `signing.sign_opcert_manifest` and `sshsig.sign` (the DSSE keyid that must match the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry) is never defined as a schema field or configuration source for the server. The plan specifies how the key is fetched and materialized, but not how the server knows its own identity string.
findings.location: Context — Signing key storage + materialization; HTTP handler contract
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the IaC/handler spec: 'The server's `principal` (env_id) is supplied via an environment variable `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` set by the IaC stack; its value MUST match the `env_id` entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`. The IaC test asserts this variable is declared.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) that asserts `merged_log_commit` in the response equals the authoritative commit the server fetched — not a client-supplied or hard-coded value.
findings.finding: The `merged_log_commit` field appears in the response schema (Context) but no acceptance criterion verifies it is populated from authoritative sources or tests its correctness — it is an orphaned described deliverable.
findings.location: Context — HTTP handler contract, response schema: `"merged_log_commit": str`
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: after the doctored-fingerprint test, also assert that `merged_log_commit` in the response equals the HEAD of the tickets branch (or the authoritative commit the server resolved), not any client-supplied value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit constraint to the key materialization section stating that the Ed25519 private key stored in Secrets Manager must be a passphrase-free OpenSSH private key (no `-p` passphrase), because `sshsig.sign()` invokes `ssh-keygen -Y sign` with no passphrase-supply mechanism.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 key materialization protocol never states that the key stored in Secrets Manager must be passphrase-free. The existing `sshsig.sign()` invokes `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <path>` (confirmed: src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:80-86) with no mechanism to supply a passphrase; a passphrase-protected key would cause the subprocess to hang or fail non-obviously at signing time.
findings.location: Context — Signing key storage + materialization; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Signing key storage + materialization' bullet: 'The key stored in Secrets Manager MUST be a passphrase-free OpenSSH Ed25519 private key (generated with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""`); `sshsig.sign()` has no passphrase-supply path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC2's test mechanism: specify that the test mocks/stubs the authoritative fetch to return a known fingerprint that differs from any client-supplied value, then asserts the signed op-cert binds the mocked-authoritative fingerprint.
findings.finding: AC2 describes the integrity test as 'the handler is invoked with a doctored `material_fingerprint`', but the request schema (Context) explicitly excludes `material_fingerprint` as a client-supplied field — the mechanism by which the doctored value reaches the handler is unspecified, leaving ambiguity about what the test actually exercises.
findings.location: AC2 (integrity test) vs. Context — HTTP handler contract, request schema
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC2's setup clause to: 'the authoritative ticket fetch is stubbed to return a specific known fingerprint F_auth; the request includes an extra field `material_fingerprint` = F_doctored ≠ F_auth; the test asserts the signed op-cert's bound fingerprint equals F_auth.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC3 whether `sshsig.sign` / `ssh-keygen` is invoked for real or mocked; if mocked, document why the mock is faithful to production behavior AND add a separate integration-level assertion (or container-image test) that a real ssh-keygen invocation leaves no temp file, even on subprocess failure.
findings.finding: The AC3 signing test (asserts no key file remains after signing) does not specify whether `sshsig.sign` / `ssh-keygen` is invoked for real or mocked; if the ssh-keygen subprocess is mocked/stubbed, the actual OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 subprocess path — the behavior the test is designed to validate — is never exercised, making the test a proxy-validation that goes green even if real ssh-keygen invocation is broken or the finally-block path diverges under a real subprocess exit.
findings.location: AC3 (signing path / temp file deletion test)
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC3: 'The signing integration test invokes real ssh-keygen (using the test container image) and asserts: (a) the temp file is 0600-mode before signing, (b) the temp file does not exist after the call (success path), and (c) the temp file does not exist after the call when ssh-keygen exits non-zero (failure path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `prevent_destroy = true` and an explicit `recovery_window_in_days` (≥ 7) to the Secrets Manager secret IaC declaration for the Ed25519 signing key, matching the `prevent_destroy` pattern already established for the EBS data volume and EIP.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no `prevent_destroy` (or equivalent safeguard) on the AWS Secrets Manager secret holding the Ed25519 signing key. This secret is the root of trust for all op-cert signatures — accidental `terraform destroy`, resource recreation, or a misconfigured apply would permanently destroy it, invalidating the pinned public key and breaking verification for all existing op-certs.
findings.location: AC#4 — 'The Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the IaC for `aws_secretsmanager_secret.opcert_signing_key`, add:
```hcl
lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }
recovery_window_in_days = 14
```

findings.finding: The plan introduces AWS Secrets Manager as a second secret-storage service alongside the existing AWS SSM SecureString infrastructure. All 15 current production secrets live in SSM SecureString (infra/terraform/ssm.tf), the IAM grants are already scoped to SSM (`rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` on `/rebar/prod/*`), and boto3's `ssm.get_parameter` is the established retrieval pattern. Secrets Manager is a distinct AWS service requiring a separate IAM action (`secretsmanager:GetSecretValue`), a new KMS CMK (vs the AWS-managed SSM key), different SDK calls, and separate operator runbooks — none of which exist in the codebase today. The plan gives no justification for why SSM SecureString cannot hold the Ed25519 key, which would reuse existing IAM grants, Terraform patterns, and operator tooling.
findings.location: AWS infra decisions section + AC bullet (c): 'the Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare the Ed25519 signing key as `aws_ssm_parameter` SecureString under `/rebar/prod/opcert-signing-key` (matching the existing `local.rebar_secret_params` pattern in ssm.tf) and grant `ssm:GetParameter` + `kms:Decrypt via ssm kms:ViaService` on the server's execution role — exactly the grant already present in iam.tf. This eliminates the new KMS CMK, the Secrets Manager resource, and the new `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` IAM action, while keeping the materialization-to-temp-file pattern unchanged.

findings.finding: The plan chooses AWS Secrets Manager for the Ed25519 signing key without considering or justifying the deviation from the established pattern. Every other production secret in this codebase uses AWS SSM Parameter Store SecureString (confirmed: ssm.tf declares all 14 production secrets as aws_ssm_parameter SecureString resources with KMS-at-rest encryption and lifecycle ignore_changes). AWS SSM SecureString already provides KMS-encrypted-at-rest storage, fine-grained IAM read policies, and the proven least-privilege pattern the plan also requires — Secrets Manager is a distinct AWS service with a different API and a different pricing model. The plan introduces Secrets Manager as a greenfield dependency with zero existing usage in infra/terraform/ without explaining why SSM SecureString is insufficient for this use case.
findings.location: Context section — 'Signing key storage + materialization. The Ed25519 private key material lives in AWS Secrets Manager, encrypted at rest by a KMS CMK.' / infra/terraform/ssm.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rationale in the Context section, e.g. 'Secrets Manager is chosen over SSM SecureString because [specific reason, e.g. built-in rotation API / resource-level policies / cross-account access].' If no such reason exists, replace with an SSM SecureString slot following the pattern in ssm.tf and extend the existing aws_iam_role_policy.ssm_params_read document.

findings.finding: The plan stores the Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager (a new AWS service not present anywhere in the existing Terraform) while the established pattern for all other secrets — including another Ed25519 key (`/rebar/prod/gerrit-ssh-host-ed25519-key`) — is SSM SecureString with KMS-CMK encryption (`infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, `infra/terraform/iam.tf`). Introducing a second secrets-storage service for a single key that the existing SSM SecureString pattern already covers at the same security profile (KMS-encrypted at rest, IAM-scoped read, no plaintext in Terraform state) is a config-surface proliferation with no stated functional justification.
findings.location: Context section / AC item 3 ('the Ed25519 signing key material stored in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add the signing key as a new entry in `local.rebar_secret_params` in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (e.g. `/rebar/prod/opcert-signing-ed25519-key`) using the existing `aws_ssm_parameter` resource with `type = "SecureString"`. Scope the new least-privilege IAM policy to `ssm:GetParameter` on that one path + `kms:Decrypt` via `kms:ViaService`, mirroring `data.aws_iam_policy_document.ssm_params_read` already in `iam.tf`. No new AWS service required.

findings.finding: The plan specifies AWS Secrets Manager for the Ed25519 signing key, but the existing repo infra exclusively uses SSM Parameter Store SecureStrings (ssm.tf lists all secrets as `aws_ssm_parameter` resources). Secrets Manager is a new, unused service surface with different IAM actions (`secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` vs `ssm:GetParameter`), a different KMS-CMK integration path, and a different rotation API. No `aws_secretsmanager_secret` IaC stub exists. The plan does not acknowledge this divergence from the established pattern or justify the new service.
findings.location: AC line 3(c) — 'Ed25519 signing key material stored in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the handler's sub-step failure protocol: specify what HTTP status code and response body shape (or extended `verdict` enum value, e.g., `"ERROR"`) the handler returns when the gate produces INDETERMINATE, when signing fails (`SshKeygenUnavailable`, Secrets Manager timeout), or when the authoritative fetch fails.
findings.finding: No fallback is defined for a gate-run sub-step failure inside the trusted server handler (e.g., LLM unavailable, `SshKeygenUnavailable` raised, Secrets Manager fetch timeout). The response schema has only `"verdict": "PASS"|"FAIL"` with no `INDETERMINATE` or error state, leaving the failure protocol for sub-steps completely unspecified.
findings.location: Context — HTTP handler contract / Response schema; AC1; AC4

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'Lambda container image OR ECS/Fargate' fork in the plan: pick one compute target, state it as the decision, and update AC4 to name the specific IaC resource type (e.g. `AWS::Lambda::Function` with `PackageType: Image` or `AWS::ECS::TaskDefinition`).
findings.finding: The plan specifies the compute environment as 'a Lambda container image OR ECS/Fargate task' without deciding which; the executor must choose, and the two options produce substantially different IaC (different resource types, API Gateway integration types, IAM execution roles). This is a defaultable gap that should be resolved in the plan.
findings.location: Compute environment section; AC4 (IaC declaration)
findings.suggested_fix: If gate runs are short (< 2 min) and invocations are infrequent, default to Lambda container image (simpler IaC, no cluster management, pay-per-invocation). State: 'Compute: Lambda container image (PackageType: Image). API Gateway Lambda proxy integration.' and update AC4 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare a mitigating control for SIGKILL-resistant temp-key cleanup: either (a) rely on Lambda container-level ephemeral storage isolation (document the guarantee that /tmp is NOT shared across distinct Lambda function invocations from different users), or (b) add a startup-time scan that unlinks any leftover key temps on cold/warm start, or (c) restructure to use a named temporary directory that is force-purged on container init.
findings.finding: The plan relies solely on a Python `finally` block to delete the materialized Ed25519 private key temp file after signing. A forceful container kill (Lambda execution timeout → SIGKILL, OOM kill) bypasses `finally`, leaving the private key on disk. In a Lambda warm-container reuse scenario, the file persists into the next invocation — potentially readable by whoever triggers the next request under the same container instance. The plan declares no mitigating control (e.g., per-invocation /tmp ephemeral isolation, a startup cleanup scan for stale key temps, or a TemporaryDirectory that the OS reclaims on exec death).
findings.location: Context → 'Signing key storage + materialization' paragraph; AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 3: 'A startup hook (e.g., module-level init or Lambda handler preamble) scans the temp directory and unlinks any stale key files from a prior SIGKILL'd invocation before accepting a new request; the test asserts no key file remains even when the signing subprocess is killed mid-flight (simulated via monkeypatch).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full Ed25519 key lifecycle: who generates the keypair and seeds the Secrets Manager secret (and how — e.g. a one-time operator script), whether the IaC creates a placeholder or expects a pre-seeded secret, and specify a key-rotation procedure that keeps old op-cert verifications working (e.g. a public-key registry or version tag on the secret).
findings.finding: The lifecycle of the Ed25519 signing key in Secrets Manager is underspecified: the plan does not state who generates the key pair and seeds the secret value (operator? IaC? CI?), nor is there a key-rotation or retirement path — if the key is compromised or must be rotated, old op-certs become unverifiable without a versioning scheme.
findings.location: AWS infra decisions / Signing key storage section; AC item 3 and AC item 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `infra/keygen.sh` (or equivalent CDK custom resource) that: (1) generates a new Ed25519 keypair with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519`, (2) stores the private key in Secrets Manager under the declared secret ARN, and (3) records the public key in a versioned `trusted_keys/` directory in the repo. For rotation, add a secret version tag and update the public-key registry — old op-certs remain verifiable against prior public-key versions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in AC#4 (and the IaC) the network isolation model for the signing container: specify VPC placement (private subnet, no public IP), the security group rules (no inbound except from the API Gateway VPC endpoint or equivalent), and that no Lambda function URL is enabled.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify network isolation for the signing container (Lambda VPC configuration or ECS task security group / subnet placement). API Gateway is stated as the entry point, but if the container is not placed in a private subnet with no public inbound route — or if Lambda is not VPC-bound with an appropriate security group — the container runtime may be directly reachable, bypassing the SigV4 authentication layer.
findings.location: Context section — 'fronted by AWS API Gateway'; AC#4(a)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dispatch protocol table in the HTTP handler contract section mapping each `kind` value to the library entry-point it invokes (e.g., `completion-verifier` → `rebar.llm.verify_completion(...)`, `plan-review` → `rebar.llm.review_plan(...)`) and specify any per-kind conditional signing behavior.
findings.finding: The `kind` field enum (`"completion-verifier"|"plan-review"`) is referenced in the request schema but no dispatch protocol is specified: the plan never states which library entry-point the handler calls for each kind value. The existing entry-points are `rebar.llm.verify_completion` and `rebar.llm.review_plan` (confirmed in codebase), but an implementer cannot derive the dispatch mapping from the plan text.
findings.location: Context — HTTP handler contract / Request schema; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Context's 'HTTP handler contract' section: 'The handler dispatches on `kind`: `completion-verifier` → `rebar.llm.verify_completion(ticket_id, ref=commit, source="attested")`; `plan-review` → `rebar.llm.review_plan(ticket_id, ref=commit, source="attested", sign=True)`. An unknown `kind` returns HTTP 422.'

findings.finding: The plan does not close the network-reachability of the underlying compute service. The plan says the HTTP service is 'fronted by AWS API Gateway' but does not state whether the compute listener (Lambda function URL, ALB, or direct HTTP port) is inaccessible except via API Gateway. If the compute endpoint is independently reachable (e.g., a Lambda function URL, an ALB with open SG, or a public EC2 port), its human/admin auth is unspecified — the API Gateway SigV4 restriction only guards the Gateway's own endpoint.
findings.location: AC bullet 2 ('library-mode HTTP service … fronted by AWS API Gateway'); AWS infra decisions section
findings.suggested_fix: For Lambda: 'No function URL is provisioned; the Lambda resource policy grants lambda:InvokeFunction only to the API Gateway execution role ARN.' For EC2/ECS: 'Security group allows inbound only from the API Gateway managed prefix list or VPC endpoint; no public port is open.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the handler contract and AC to define error response shapes and HTTP status codes for key non-happy-path cases (remote fetch failure → 502 + reason, invalid commit → 400, Secrets Manager unavailable → 503), and add at least one negative-path test to AC item 1.
findings.finding: The handler's non-happy-path HTTP behavior is unspecified: the response schema covers only the success shape; error states (authoritative-remote fetch failure, invalid/unknown commit, Secrets Manager unavailability, malformed request) have no defined HTTP status codes or error response bodies.
findings.location: HTTP handler contract section; AC item 1 (request/response round-trip test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an error response schema: `{"error": "FETCH_FAILED"|"INVALID_COMMIT"|"SIGNING_UNAVAILABLE"|"BAD_REQUEST", "detail": str}` with mapped HTTP status codes (400/502/503), and add an AC sub-item: 'A request with an unknown commit SHA returns HTTP 400 with error=INVALID_COMMIT.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or CLAUDE.md entry) capturing the key architectural decisions: Ed25519/SSHSIG via temp-file materialization (not KMS managed signing), library-mode HTTP service pattern, container requirement for OpenSSH ≥ 8.9, and SigV4 IAM auth at API Gateway.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architecture (trusted remote execution path, Ed25519 SSHSIG temp-file materialization, SigV4 IAM auth, container SSHSIG signing service) but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update to capture these decisions for future maintainers.
findings.location: Context section; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-trusted-remote-execution-path.md` recording: (1) why KMS managed signing is excluded (ECDSA/RSA only), (2) temp-file materialization pattern and its security contract, (3) container-only deploy requirement for OpenSSH ≥ 8.9, (4) library-mode HTTP vs stdio-MCP rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the two hard external calls (Secrets Manager fetch + git remote fetch): define timeouts, at least one retry with backoff, and a structured error response (e.g., `{"error": "dependency_unavailable", "detail": ...}`) so callers and operators can distinguish gate failures from infra failures.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling, retry, or timeout for the two hard external calls in the hot signing path: (a) AWS Secrets Manager fetch and (b) authoritative git remote fetch. If either is slow or unavailable, the request hangs or fails opaquely with no defined behavior.
findings.location: Context section ('materializes it to a 0600-mode temp file'); HTTP handler contract section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: the HTTP handler must emit structured logs (request outcome, dependency call results, temp-file cleanup status) and at minimum one metric or health-check endpoint so operators can detect and diagnose signing-path failures.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new production service (trusted signing server) with no observability specification: no structured logging, metrics, distributed tracing, or alerting for the new failure points (Secrets Manager fetch, git fetch, signing subprocess, key-file cleanup).
findings.location: HTTP handler contract section; AC1–AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC-level test requirements for at least: (a) invalid/non-existent `commit` ref returns a defined error response, (b) unknown `kind` value returns a defined error, (c) authoritative-fetch failure (network error or 404) returns a graceful error response without leaking key material, and (d) malformed/missing JSON fields return a 4xx with a documented error shape.
findings.finding: The new HTTP handler is a user-facing flow but the plan specifies only happy-path and one integrity test; no failure/timeout/invalid-input paths are required by any AC — e.g., malformed `commit` ref (non-existent, bad SHA), unknown `kind` value, Secrets Manager fetch failure, authoritative ticket fetch network error, or missing required JSON fields.
findings.location: AC1 (handler round-trip test) and AC2 (integrity test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a failure-paths AC: 'The handler returns a structured error response (non-2xx, JSON body with `error` field) for: unknown `kind`, non-existent `commit`, authoritative-fetch failure, and missing required fields; tests assert no temp key file remains in each failure case.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific library entry-points the HTTP handler calls to run each gate kind (`plan-review` / `completion-verifier`) and confirm they are (or will be) side-effect-free for remote invocation (no ticket-mutating steps the server should not perform).
findings.finding: The plan says the handler 'runs the gate' for `completion-verifier` and `plan-review` kinds but never names the library entry-points it must call to do so. The existing gate machinery lives in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (plan-review) and `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` / `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` (completion-verifier), and `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.current_material_fingerprint` is the function that computes the material fingerprint. None of these are named as consumers/integration targets in the plan, leaving the implementer without a concrete API surface to call.
findings.location: Context / HTTP handler contract — 'runs the gate, computes the material fingerprint ITSELF'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC#4 (and the IaC) a requirement for API Gateway access logging (to a CloudWatch log group), execution logs for the Lambda/ECS task, and at least one CloudWatch alarm (e.g. 5xx rate or invocation errors) wired to the existing SNS alert topic.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (access logs, CloudWatch metrics, or alarms) for the new API Gateway endpoint or the signing container. This is a security-critical endpoint that materializes a private key and signs op-certs; without an audit trail of who invoked it and what verdicts were signed, incident response and abuse detection are impossible.
findings.location: AC#4 — IaC declaration requirements; plan Context section describing the HTTP handler

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document a rollback procedure in `infra/README` or the plan (e.g. 'redeploy prior container image tag' or 'cdk deploy --rollback'), and require the operator-attested AC to record the previous-known-good image tag or stack version alongside the deploy evidence.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no rollback path for the deployed AWS service: if the container image or handler is broken post-deploy, there is no stated mechanism (prior image tag, stack rollback command, or traffic cut-back) to quickly restore a working state.
findings.location: AC item 6 (operator-attested deployment); AWS infra decisions section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one code-level integration test (e.g., using a local bare git repo as the authoritative remote, or a test-environment remote) that exercises the real fetch path in AC1/AC2, so the handler's fetch behavior is codebase-verifiable independent of operator attestation.
findings.finding: AC1's test uses a 'fake authoritative source' to validate the handler, meaning the handler's core new security behavior — fetching ticket state and code-at-commit from real authoritative remotes ITSELF — is exercised only through a canned substitute that bypasses the actual remote fetch; AC6 is operator-attested (not a named child ticket) and is thus not a codebase-verifiable guarantee, leaving the live remote-fetch path untested by any code-level criterion.
findings.location: AC1 (round-trip test) and AC6 (operator-attested deployment)
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC1: additionally require a test against a local bare git repo (acting as the authoritative remote) that confirms the handler correctly resolves the commit and ticket state via real git operations, distinct from the fake-source round-trip test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly note that the new op-cert server Dockerfile diverges from `Dockerfile.reviewbot` by adding `openssh-client` (≥ 8.9) — the reviewbot pattern does NOT install OpenSSH, so the new Dockerfile is not a simple copy.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `review_bot` pattern is 'proven' as the template for the new library-mode HTTP service, but the existing `Dockerfile.reviewbot` — the concrete artifact of that pattern — does NOT install OpenSSH at all (only `git`, `curl`, `ca-certificates`). The new op-cert server requires OpenSSH >= 8.9 for SSHSIG signing. If implementers mirror Dockerfile.reviewbot without noticing this gap, the new container will lack `ssh-keygen` entirely, causing every signing call to raise `SshKeygenUnavailable`.
findings.location: Context section — 'mirroring the proven `review_bot` pattern'; AC-4d — 'a container image whose Dockerfile installs OpenSSH ≥ 8.9'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Context or AC-4d: 'The new Dockerfile differs from Dockerfile.reviewbot in that it must also `apt-get install -y openssh-client` (or equivalent) to satisfy the SSHSIG ≥ 8.9 requirement; Dockerfile.reviewbot does not include this step.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or note in AC-4d) that the new container Dockerfile installs `openssh-client` (which provides both `ssh` and `ssh-keygen`) so that `ensure_available()`'s `ssh -V` probe and `sshsig.sign`'s `ssh-keygen -Y sign` call both resolve; the current `ssh_keygen_version()` implementation uses `ssh`, not `ssh-keygen`, for version detection.
findings.finding: The plan describes `ensure_available()` as checking for 'ssh-keygen' availability, but the actual implementation (`ssh_keygen_version()`) detects the version by running `ssh -V` (the SSH client binary), not `ssh-keygen -V`. In a minimal container image it is possible to install `ssh-keygen` without the `ssh` client (or vice versa), meaning the version check could pass even if `ssh-keygen` is absent, or fail even if it is present. The IaC test mandated by AC-4d ('asserts the Dockerfile/image installs OpenSSH') rests on the assumption that installing OpenSSH satisfies the `ensure_available()` probe, which is accurate for standard packages — but the implementation's use of `ssh -V` (not `ssh-keygen -V`) is an unbacked detail the plan's AC test description does not account for.
findings.location: Context section — 'sshsig.ensure_available() hard-requires OpenSSH ≥ 8.9'; AC-4d

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific new Terraform file path(s) under `infra/terraform/` (or a named sibling) where the opcert trusted-server IaC resources will live, so the AC4 test has a concrete file to assert against.
findings.finding: The plan names `infra/` as the location for all new IaC but the repo's existing IaC lives specifically in `infra/terraform/`. No new Terraform files (API Gateway, Secrets Manager, Lambda/ECS container image, IAM execution role) exist there yet. The plan does not name any specific new file paths under `infra/terraform/`, leaving it ambiguous whether the implementer should add to `infra/terraform/` or create a sibling directory. This is a CREATION gap in file-path enumeration.
findings.location: AC bullet 4 — 'The AWS IaC (`infra/`) declares…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'New IaC files: `infra/terraform/opcert_server.tf` (API Gateway + Lambda/ECS), `infra/terraform/opcert_iam.tf` (execution role + secret read policy), `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (container image with OpenSSH ≥ 8.9).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in the plan or an ADR) why in-process Ed25519 signing (e.g. Python `cryptography` library) was not used to eliminate temp-file key materialization, or switch to in-process signing if no compelling reason to keep the subprocess/file-path approach exists.
findings.finding: The plan commits to materializing the Ed25519 private key to a transient temp file (a novel, security-sensitive pattern) because `sshsig.sign` requires a file path — but never documents that the in-memory Ed25519 signing alternative (e.g. Python's `cryptography` library, which supports Ed25519 signing without shelling out and without touching the filesystem) was evaluated and rejected. The prior art for in-process signing is directly applicable here and would eliminate the disk-exposure window entirely.
findings.location: Context section: 'Signing key storage + materialization' / AC3: 'The signing path materializes the Secrets Manager Ed25519 secret to a 0600 temp file'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add API Gateway throttling (stage-level burst + rate limits, or a usage plan) to AC#4 and the IaC, and state the expected concurrency model for the signing handler (e.g. reserved concurrency of 1 for Lambda, or a single Fargate task).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify API Gateway throttling (rate limits / usage plan) for the signing endpoint. Even with IAM authentication, an admin credential that issues many requests rapidly would trigger many signing operations and corresponding key-materialization cycles, creating unnecessary exposure of the temp-key pattern at scale.
findings.location: AC#4(a) — 'endpoint authentication via AWS IAM SigV4 at API Gateway'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly call out (or justify the absence of) a lower environment (dev/stage) for the signing service IaC, or document the risk-acceptance rationale for deploying directly to the single-account prod workspace.
findings.finding: The plan does not mention dev/stage/prod environment separation for the new signing service. The existing infra is single-environment ('prod'). Adding a security-critical key-signing endpoint to the same account and Terraform workspace, without a separate lower environment for testing, means IaC changes are only validated in production.
findings.location: AC#4 — IaC declaration; plan Context section

findings.finding: The HTTP handler's request/response schema is never defined, so the plan's core decision rule ('ignore client-provided state, recompute from authoritative sources') cannot be correctly implemented or verified — an implementer cannot determine which fields are 'client inputs to be ignored' vs. required routing parameters.
findings.location: AC2 — integrity test / Transport section (library-mode HTTP handler)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Handler schema' subsection to the plan specifying the request body (e.g., `{ticket_id: str, manifest: list[str]}` as the only client inputs) and explicitly noting that `material_fingerprint` and `merged_log_commit` are always server-derived and never read from the request.

findings.finding: The signing mechanism requires `ssh-keygen ≥ 8.9` to be present in the server's runtime environment (sshsig.py:60-71 enforces this via `ensure_available()`), but the plan names no compute environment (Lambda, ECS, EC2 container) for the new trusted server and provides no specification that the deployment image/environment installs OpenSSH. The analogous review_bot Dockerfile (`infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`) — which the plan explicitly mirrors — uses `python:3.12-slim` and installs only `git curl ca-certificates`, not `openssh-client`. Without an explicit provision step, the signing call would fail closed with `SshKeygenUnavailable` at every request in production.
findings.location: Context section (AWS infra decisions) / AC3 (signing path) / infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot line 35
findings.suggested_fix: Add `openssh-client` to the trusted server's Dockerfile (e.g. `apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends git curl ca-certificates openssh-client`) and add an AC or IaC note that the deployment image must include OpenSSH ≥ 8.9. Also add a startup `sshsig.ensure_available()` call so the server refuses to boot rather than failing per-request.

findings.finding: The plan asserts ssh-keygen -Y sign will be invokable in the trusted server's AWS runtime, but does not specify the execution environment (Lambda, ECS/Fargate, EC2) or require openssh-client in the container image. The reference pattern (Dockerfile.reviewbot) installs only git/curl/ca-certificates — openssh-client is absent, so ssh-keygen would be missing at runtime and sshsig.ensure_available() would raise SshKeygenUnavailable.
findings.location: Context / AWS infra decisions section; AC3 (signing path); infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot
findings.suggested_fix: Add `openssh-client` to the trusted server's Dockerfile (or equivalent): `apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends openssh-client` and add a build-time smoke test `RUN ssh-keygen -Y sign --help 2>&1 | grep -q sign`. Also add a startup assertion calling `sshsig.ensure_available()` in the HTTP handler's lifespan so a misconfigured deployment fails fast rather than at first signing request.

findings.finding: AC2's integrity test does not specify whether the authoritative remote fetch is live or mocked. If the fetch is satisfied by a test double (stub returning canned ticket data), the test validates the recomputation and signing logic but never exercises the actual remote-fetch code path — the core trust guarantee of this service. The plan provides no rationale explaining why a stub is faithful to production for this boundary.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2

findings.finding: AC1's test ('a test asserts a client-supplied doctored input does NOT change the signed verdict') can be satisfied by an offline unit test that stubs/mocks the authoritative remote fetch, never exercising the actual network boundary to ticket state or repo. This is the proxy-validation anti-pattern: the live path — 'fetch ticket state + code-at-commit from authoritative remotes itself' — is the core new behavior, and offline-green coverage that never connects to a real remote lets the work close green while the live fetch path is broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 to read: '…a test (with a live or realistic-remote fixture) confirms the server fetches ticket state and code-at-commit itself and a client-supplied doctored payload does NOT change the signed verdict; the live-fetch path is exercised end-to-end, not only through mocked stubs.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
